content_of_post_to_comment:
Every time I wanted to build a habit, I'd go all in.

I'd make a plan, promise myself I'd stick to it, and for a few days I'd actually do pretty well.

Then I'd miss one day.

Sometimes I was just tired. Sometimes work got in the way. Sometimes I just didn't feel like doing it.

The weird part wasn't missing the day.

It was what happened after.

I'd tell myself I had already messed it up, so I might as well start again next Monday.

I honestly repeated that cycle for years.

At some point I realized I wasn't struggling with habits as much as I was struggling with the idea of restarting.

Missing one day isn't what kept me stuck.

Starting from zero every single time did.

Once I stopped treating every mistake like a reset button, staying consistent became a lot easier.

Not because I suddenly had more motivation.

Just because I finally stopped making things harder than they needed to be.

I found an article a while back that explained this way better than I could. It was the first time I felt like someone described exactly what I had been doing for years.

Has anyone else realized that restarting was actually the biggest problem?
comment: Falling into the 'start over Monday' loop is what gets me too, especially with streak apps making one missed day feel like everything’s broken. I’ve had better luck just tracking total sessions per week instead of perfect streaks, so a bad day doesn’t turn into a full stop. Curious if you ever switched how you measured progress?
